Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: 1. Understand User Goal: The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that: Takes an event_log table with case_id, activity, timestamp. Defines a process variant as the ordered sequence of activities per case sorted by timestamp. Groups cases by this sequence to find unique variants. Counts cases per variant. Finds the top K variants by frequency. Returns all events from the original event_log that belong to cases matching these top K variants. Must handle K as a parameter or assume its provided.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: 2. Key Steps in SQL: Step 1: Order events per case by timestamp and aggregate activities into a sequence/array/string. Step 2: Count occurrences of each sequence variant. Step 3: Rank/limit to top K variants. Step 4: Join/filter the original event log to only include cases in those top K variants. Step 5: Return the filtered events, presumably ordered appropriately.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: 3. DuckDB Specifics: DuckDB has excellent array/list functions. list_agg(activity ORDER BY timestamp) is perfect for creating the sequence per case. Alternatively string_agg(activity -> ORDER BY timestamp) works, but arrays are more robust and standard in DuckDB. Ill use list_agg. DuckDB supports QUALIFY, window functions, CTEs, and parameterization. Ill use a CTE-based approach for clarity. K needs to be a parameter. Ill use :K.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Step 1 & 2: Create variant sequences and count them WITH case_variants AS ( SELECT case_id, list_agg(activity ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant_seq FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id ), variant_counts AS ( SELECT variant_seq, COUNT(case_id) AS case_count FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant_seq ), top_variants AS ( SELECT variant_seq FROM variant_counts ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT :K -- K is a parameter )

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 3 & 4: Filter original log Now I need to join event_log with top_variants. But top_variants only has variant_seq. I need to know which case_ids belong to those variants. I can either: a) Join case_variants with top_variants to get the case_ids, then join back to event_log. b) Use a window function or IN clause. c) Combine steps efficiently. Lets refine

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: WITH case_variants AS ( SELECT case_id, list_agg(activity ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant_seq FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id ), variant_counts AS ( SELECT variant_seq, COUNT(*) AS case_count FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant_seq ), top_variant_seqs AS ( SELECT variant_seq FROM variant_counts ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT :K ), top_case_ids AS ( SELECT cv.case_id FROM case_variants cv JOIN top_variant_seqs tvs ON cv.variant_seq = tvs.variant_seq ) SELECT e.* FROM event_log e JOIN top_case_ids tci ON e.case_id = tci.case_id ORDER BY e.case_id, e.timestamp;

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This works, but DuckDB has a nice feature: list_agg returns a LIST. Comparing lists is supported in DuckDB. So cv.variant_seq = tvs.variant_seq works. Ill stick with list_agg. Lets test mentally: case_variants groups by case_id, creates ordered list. variant_counts groups by variant_seq, counts. top_variant_seqs orders by count desc, limits to K. top_case_ids joins back to get case_ids. Final SELECT joins event_log with top_case_ids. This is solid.

Name: Backtracking - IND
Text: Can I simplify? Yes, using QUALIFY or window functions might reduce CTEs, but CTEs are clearer. Lets see if I can do it in fewer steps or more elegantly. Ill keep it as is for readability. LIMIT in a CTE might not be allowed in some SQL dialects, but DuckDB supports it.

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: One potential issue: list_agg order might not be deterministic if timestamps are identical. The prompt says ordered sequence of activities determined by sorting events within that case by their timestamp. If timestamps tie, the order might be arbitrary, but thats acceptable unless specified otherwise. Ill stick to ORDER BY timestamp.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets verify DuckDB syntax for list_agg: list_agg(expression ORDER BY sort_expression) is valid. List comparison: list1 = list2 works in DuckDB. Parameter: :K. Correct. Final check of the prompt requirements: All covered.
